value.answer:
A **star schema** (also known as the Kimball approach or dimensional modeling) is a structural design used in the data warehouse layer to organize information specifically for analytics [1, 2]. Its visual layout resembles a star, featuring one centralized table that connects outward to several surrounding tables [3-5].

It is built using two primary components:

*   **The Fact Table (The Center):** Sitting at the core of the star, the fact table represents a core **business action** or "verb", such as a sale, an invoice sent, or a transaction [6-8]. Fact tables are designed to strictly hold the **numeric metrics** you want to aggregate (like revenue, taxes, or quantity) and the keys (IDs) required to join to the surrounding dimension tables [8-10]. Fact tables should always be built at the **lowest possible granularity** to give you the most flexibility to roll up and summarize data later [4, 6, 11].
*   **The Dimension Tables (The Points):** These tables surround the central fact table and provide the **descriptive context** around the business action [7, 8, 12]. Acting as the "nouns and adjectives" of the model, dimensions are typically wide, denormalized tables containing text-based attributes, booleans, strings, and dates, such as customer names, product categories, or locations [8, 13, 14]. 

In a properly designed star schema, you **always join the central fact table directly to the dimension tables** [4, 5]. The methodology strictly warns that you should **never join one dimension directly to another dimension** (often called a snowflake schema), as this can create complicated many-to-many relationships and cause accidental data duplication or fan-outs [5, 15-17]. 

Ultimately, by isolating the numerical metrics in the center and keeping the descriptions on the outside, the star schema creates a highly organized foundation that makes it incredibly easy for reporting tools to slice, dice, and summarize business data [5, 18, 19].

use is truly a single source of truth because it's representing each of these sources exactly the downside is there's a lot of joins involved which is not really ideal for column based modern data warehouses and you also have the potential for conflicting data marts depending on how they're designed and also you just don't get full access to everything from one place next we have what's called denormalized modeling and or you might just hear this referred to as dimensional modeling and that's made famous by ralph kimball and the main differentiator here is that this is where you'll see what's known as a star schema the idea here is rather than having individual normalized tables where everything is based on a single source table instead you have each of these as flattened denormalized models so there is some data redundancy within your warehouse but once you get to your analytics layer there's less joins required to get what you want and you can have it all pulled from one single sourc

Do you want to just overwrite that value or do you track them as individual records and have dates around when they were active? That's what a slowly changing dimensions all about. There are strategies for handling that. That can get a little tricky and I don't want to get too far in the weeds on that, but those are slowly changing dimensions. And then here's a visual. I think sometimes visuals help. Think about a fact table as the core concept, the business action, and then the dimensions are connected to it through keys. So instead of storing all of the descriptive values in here, you only store the key and then when you want to get those values, you join to the dimensions. I think of data modeling a lot like organizing your room in a way. You know, you don't have to, but it's a lot easier when you have a structure and you have it organized. You know where things are.

You know how things are sorted and you can very easily grab things and put it together and you just keep it organized so you can use it more effectively as opposed to not having it at all. But the fact table is in the middle and then you connect to the dimensions and this visual here is why it's called a star schema. It kind of looks like a star. So, here's an example fact order transactions. We're we're looking at a really granular level in this case. This is where we're going to keep all of the transaction metrics and then the keys. And here are some of the dimensions around this example situation. So maybe we want to see transaction by product for the specific order itself. Who was a customer on it? What was the date? What employees were involved. So the fact table would have the metrics and the keys to each of these different dimensions.

Then you build your fact tables using the prefixed FCT underscore. This central node stores the core measurable actions of the business, the verbs like an invoice sent or a game played populated with specific numbers to aggregate like revenue and tax amounts. To give those metrics context, the central fact table holds surrogate keys. These keys extend outward to join directly with your descriptive dimension tables, creating the classic shape of a star schema. By isolating the metrics in the center and keeping the descriptions on the outside, you keep the math clean. And because you never join a fact table directly to another fact table, you eliminate the risk of accidental many to many relationships that cause data duplication. Even a perfectly designed star schema is useless. If the underlying records are flawed, if the source system spits out a duplicate transaction or forgets to assign a product ID, those errors will still ruin the final calculations.

So let's talk about this here when it comes to facts and dimensions. This is always a conversation that trips people up and there's always nuances to all of this so it's hard to make blanket statements but these are rules of thumb to think about. A fact table is really the core business action. So if you look at this here I think of it like a verb. What was the thing that happened in the business? It was a sale. It was a game was played. It was an invoice was sent. Those are things that happened. And so that's what a fact table represents. And then a dimension is more of the context around it. It's not the action. It's the information and description. around it. So, who was the customer? What was the product? What was the location? I think of these more like adjectives and nouns. They're describing the business action that took place. When you think about back to the pillars, the insights, the reporting ultimately, we're not just creating all of this stuff for the sake of it.

You then save this view using the prefix STG underscore so everyone knows immediately what level of the pipeline they are looking at. There is a strict rule in this phase. Absolutely no table joins. This layer exists to enforce the dry principle. Don't repeat yourself. If a column needs rounding or a new name, you make the fix exactly once in the staging view. Because every downstream query pulls from this staging model instead of the raw table, that single correction automatically flows through the pipeline, eliminating repeated logic errors. Now that the data is clean, it moves into the warehouse layer. This is the structural core of your pipeline where you finally apply business logic to those staging views and organize the records into a dimensional star schema. These hold the descriptive context of your business, the nouns and adjectives like specific customers, employee names, or product categories.

Sometimes there are just oneoff descriptive values that don't require a full dimension. You don't care to track that whole concept by itself. It's not relevant to any other fact.

e main ways that this can manifest into a problem is that you accidentally can make your life more difficult or leave out data because you're not low enough for example let's say you make a fact table on orders so you have a fact order table but then in the future somebody wants to see individual line item information on the order so maybe there's order line items in that scenario the order line items is a lower granularity than the order because there's multiple of them within an order and then even lower than that you might even be able to go to transactions so maybe there's multiple transactions per line item now this isn't always the case and sometimes you can have both granularities but generally speaking the lower granularity you can do it's going to give you more options it's give you more flexibility because you can then roll things up for example you could take the sum of all the order line item metrics and it would equal out what you had in the full order metric and you can s

lly another answer that I think is important to share because it will highlight the differences a lot of times between different database designs which can be really important to understand especially as an engineer when you're modeling things in this scenario again I'm just looking over here at the notes their question was wouldn't it make sense to create more of a snowflake schema where the dim product table would just join directly to a dim product type table rather than through the fact and the reasoning for that was because that's what the data itself is saying so shouldn't you just model it around that so like most things in data it's not illegal you can do it but in my opinion when I hear something like that seems to align more with how an application database might be designed rather than how a data model would be designed now you could be following a different data modeling approach for example more like the traditional inm approach i have another video on comparing and talkin

g about that but in my opinion if you're going to go the fact and dimensions route it's important to maintain the concept and consistency of a star schema and to create that true star schema you pretty much always want to just join the fact directly to a dimension and not dimension two dimensions directly again there's always edge cases but you're going to get many to many relationships and things are going to blow up and you're going to get a lot of duplicates most of the time but really the main point I want to drive home really with this whole video is understanding the difference between how a database might be designed like the schemas and the tables in a database for an application so what's powering an application compared to how to design something for analytics because they are different for example if you're going to make an application database you're going to have individual tables like in that case a product type table a product table and everything is going to be really u
